claims.claim: Of the three cell chemistries tested, LFP releases the least normalized vent gas at 0.02 mol/Ah, followed by NVPF at 0.05 mol/Ah, and NMC at the highest at 0.07 mol/Ah, when triggered by two-sided overtemperature.
claims.quote: Of the three cell chemistries, LFP releases the least amount of vent gas at 0.02 mol/Ah (41% H2, 27% CO2, 8% CO), followed by NVPF at 0.05 mol/Ah (42% CO2, 17% electrolyte solvent, 15% H2 and 10% CO) and NMC at 0.07 mol/Ah (36% CO, 24% CO2, 19% H2).

claims.claim: The maximum vent gas temperature increases from NVPF (265 °C) to LFP (446 °C) to NMC (1050 °C) under two-sided overtemperature triggering.
claims.quote: The maximum vent gas temperature increases from NVPF (265 ◦C) to LFP (446 ◦C) and NMC (1050 ◦C).

claims.claim: Overcharge trigger produces the highest vent gas volume for NVPF cells at 1.23 mol (0.07 mol/Ah), and also results in the highest H2 concentration at 23% among all trigger methods tested.
claims.quote: The overcharge trigger produces the most amount of vent gas, 1.23 mol... this trigger exhibits the highest recorded H2 (23%) concentration, resulting from the side reactions, thereby increasing the explosion risk of the mixture.

claims.claim: The external short-circuit trigger produces the lowest amount of vent gas for NVPF cells at 0.14 mol, with the lowest peak vent gas temperature of 94 °C and cell surface temperature of 106 °C among all triggers tested.
claims.quote: The lowest amount of vent gas is measured for the externally shorted-circuited cell, at 0.14 mol. The peak vent gas and cell surface temperatures are the lowest recorded, at 94 ◦C and 106 ◦C, respectively.

claims.claim: The flammability window of NVPF vent gas mixture is between 7% and 28%, which is narrower than those of NMC (7% to 46%) and LFP (6% to 45%), indicating reduced explosion risk for NVPF compared to Li-ion cells.
claims.quote: This window is between 7% and 28% for the vent gas mixture of the NVPF cell. The Li-ion cells have a calculated flammability window of 7% to 46% and 6% to 45% for the NMC and LFP chemistry, respectively.

claims.claim: Increasing ventilation rate decreases gas concentration and shortens the duration of battery pack exposure to explosion risk during thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: increasing ventilation rate can decrease the gas concentration and shorten the duration of battery pack under explosion

claims.claim: Increasing cell distance reduces thermal runaway propagation speed but inhibits rapid dispersion of venting gases, causing prolonged explosion risk exposure.
claims.quote: Despite of reducing TR propagation speeds, the increase of cell distance can inhibit the rapid dispersion of venting gases, causing battery pack exposed to a prolonged explosion risk.

claims.claim: Void volume has limited effect on the duration of battery pack exposure to explosion risk during thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: a limited effect of void volume is evident for that

claims.claim: When the battery pack has no active ventilation, vented gases achieve a saturation condition and gas concentrations remain essentially constant as thermal runaway propagates through successive cells.
claims.quote: for the battery pack without active ventilation, the concentrations of H2, CO, CO2 and CH4 initially reached 0.050 g⋅L−1, 0.045 g⋅L−1, 0.038 g⋅L−1 and 0.017 g⋅L−1 when the first cell went into TR. As the safety valve of the second cell opened, the gas concentrations continued to rise, until the void of battery pack was filled with the ejected gases. In this case, the ejected gases could ultimately achieve a saturation condition that they flowed straight out after being ejected, rather than accumulating in the convergence plenum.

claims.claim: Among Z-type, U-type, and I-type air-cooling battery pack flow channel layouts, the anti-explosion performance order is Z-type > U-type > I-type, with dimensionless time indices of 0.529, 0.98, and 0.99 respectively.
claims.quote: The time index τ were calculated as 0.529, 0.98 and 0.99 for the Z-type, U-type and I-type ACBP, respectively, which is the evidence of a better safety performance for Z-type compared with other designs.

claims.claim: NMC-based cells emit higher volumes of hazardous gases and propagate thermal runaway more readily than LFP cells, indicating chemistry-dependent risk profiles.
claims.quote: comparative experiments reveal that NMC-based cells emit higher volumes of hazardous gases and propagate TR more readily than LFP cells, indicating chemistry-dependent risk profiles

claims.claim: Using conductive nails in nail penetration tests causes greater temperature spikes than non-conductive nails.
claims.quote: Abaza et al. [108], for example, found that using conductive nails in NP tests causes greater temperature spikes than non-conductive ones.

claims.claim: A study comparing various thermal runaway initiation methods found different quantities and concentrations of emitted gases even for the same cell chemistry.
claims.quote: A study comparing various TR initiation methods found different quantities and concentrations of emitted gases even for the same cell chemistry

claims.claim: LiPF6 decomposes at relatively low temperatures (around 150–200 °C), releasing highly toxic and corrosive by-products such as hydrofluoric acid.
claims.quote: The most commonly used lithium salt, lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6), offers good ionic conductivity, but it decomposes at relatively low temperatures (around 150–200 °C), releasing highly toxic and corrosive by-products such as hydrofluoric acid.

claims.claim: Total heat generation increases non-linearly with SOC in NCA-based Li-ion cells under accelerating rate calorimetry, while the onset temperature of thermal runaway decreases with increasing SOC.
claims.quote: Figure 8 presents the results of the Accelerating Rate Calorimetry (ARC) test for NCA-based Li-ion cells, demonstrating that total heat generation increases non-linearly, while the onset temperature of TR decreases with SOC.

claims.claim: For NMC prismatic cells at 100% SOC, those tested in an open setup reached peak temperatures between 665 °C and 725 °C, while those tested in a closed N2-chamber exhibited higher values ranging from 838 °C to 857 °C, suggesting more constrained heat dissipation in closed chambers.
claims.quote: For NMC prismatic cells at 100% SOC, the ones tested in an open setup reached peak temperatures between 665 °C and 725 °C, while those tested inside a closed N2-chamber exhibited much higher values, ranging from 838 °C to 857 °C. This suggests that heat dissipation is more constrained in a closed chamber, leading to higher localized temperatures.

claims.claim: In nitrogen-filled chambers during nail penetration tests, the battery reached a maximum temperature of 47 °C, compared to 122 °C in an air-filled chamber, an 88.7% increase, demonstrating the suppressive influence of inert gas on combustion-driven temperature rise.
claims.quote: Diekmann et al. [115] reported that in a nitrogen-filled chamber, the battery only reached a maximum temperature of 47 °C, compared to 122 °C in an air-filled chamber, an 88.7% increase. This finding underscores the suppressive influence of inert gases on combustion-driven temperature rise.

claims.claim: Air environments during NP tests resulted in higher CO2 and lower H2 concentrations, while N2 environments produced the opposite trend.
claims.quote: the concentrations of H2 and CO2 can differ in tests conducted in air or inert environments: air environments resulted in higher CO2 and lower H2 concentrations, while N2 environments produced the opposite trend

claims.claim: LFP cathodes at 100% SOC exhibit an onset of heat release between 180 °C and 250 °C, with peak heating occurring between 210 °C and 360 °C.
claims.quote: LFP cathodes at 100% SOC exhibit an onset of heat release between 180 °C and 250 °C, with peak heating occurring between 210 °C and 360 ◦C

claims.claim: Increased humidity within a N2 environment promoted condensation of vapor-phase products on chamber surfaces, reducing the concentration of measurable gaseous species during nail penetration tests.
claims.quote: Their results showed that increased humidity within a N2 environment promoted condensation of vapour-phase products on chamber surfaces, reducing the concentration of measurable gaseous species

claims.claim: LFP cells did not undergo failure during nail penetration tests and showed no thermal runaway, fire, or visible failure indicators such as smoke, with surface temperatures remaining between 19 °C and 46 °C.
claims.quote: Howard et al. [116] reported that during NP of LFP prismatic and pouch cells, no TR, fire, or visible failure indicators such as smoke were observed. Surface temperatures for these LFP cells remained low, ranging from 19 °C to 46 °C.

claims.claim: NMC cells reached a maximum temperature ranging from 1010 °C to 1038 °C under NP-induced thermal runaway, in contrast to LFP cells that showed no thermal runaway.
claims.quote: they found that the NMC cells reached a maximum temperature ranging from 1010 °C to 1038 °C under NP-induced TR

claims.claim: A penetration speed of 1 mm/s, much lower than the 80 mm/s suggested in SAE standards, could significantly lower standard deviations in temperature and voltage profiles and allow more stable and consistent triggering of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Diekmann et al. [115] examined the influence of penetration speed (e.g., 1 mm/s, 5 mm/s, 10 mm/s, 40 mm/s, and 80 mm/s) and found that using a penetration speed of 1 mm/s, much lower than the 80 mm/s suggested in SAE standards, could significantly lower standard deviations in temperature and voltage profiles. This slow penetration rate was found to be able to allow for more stable and consistent triggering of TR

claims.claim: Cells at 50% SOC exhibited only swelling and gas release but did not enter thermal runaway during nail penetration tests of LCO pouch cells in an open environment, while cells at 100% SOC experienced thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Diaz et al. [113] performed NP on LCO pouch cells in an open environment, and they found that only cells with 100% SOC experienced TR during NP tests, while the cells at 50% SOC exhibited only swelling and gas release but did not enter runaway.

claims.claim: Carbon monoxide concentration during thermal runaway is directly correlated to the SOC of the battery.
claims.quote: carbon monoxide has been identified as one of the primary constituents, with its concentration directly correlated to the SOC of the battery

claims.claim: LFP batteries emit significantly higher amounts of CO during thermal runaway—up to an order of magnitude greater than LCO and NMC batteries.
claims.quote: lithium iron phosphate (LFP) batteries emit significantly higher amounts–up to an order of magnitude greater than LCO and NMC batteries

claims.claim: Hard-case prismatic cells could withstand a higher internal pressure (855 kPa) before venting compared to pouch cells (411 kPa) during NP tests conducted in an inert atmosphere.
claims.quote: hard-case prismatic cells could withstand a higher internal pressure (i.e., 855 kPa) before venting, compared to pouch cells (411 kPa). As the tests were conducted in an inert atmosphere, no fires were reported.

claims.claim: With increasing SOC in NMC prismatic cells, CO concentrations became dominant over CO2, whereas H2 concentrations showed a more gradual increase with SOC, almost evening out with CO2 concentration at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: With increasing SOC, CO concentrations became dominant over CO2, whereas H2 concentrations showed a more gradual increase with SOC, almost evening out with the CO2 concentration at 100% SOC

claims.claim: The total gas generation per unit capacity for NMC prismatic cells across studies with cell capacities ranging from 60 to 157 Ah ranged from 1.77 to 2.06 L/Ah, suggesting a correlation between cell capacity and gas evolution.
claims.quote: According to these studies, the total gas generation per unit capacity ranged from 1.77 to 2.06 L/Ah, suggesting a correlation between cell capacity and gas evolution.

claims.claim: Moisture in the atmosphere can react with fluorinated decomposition products (e.g., PF5 and POF3) to form hydrofluoric acid (HF), further increasing the corrosivity and toxicity of the emission plume during thermal abuse tests.
claims.quote: moisture in the atmosphere can react with fluorinated decomposition products (e.g., PF5 and POF3) to form hydrofluoric acid (HF), further increasing the corrosivity and toxicity of the emission plume.
